title: Drag Square Handle to Extrude Cube
detail: built, but verification did not pass — review this section
summary: Drag Square Handle to Extrude Cube — The cube isn't just appearing — it's being built by sweeping the square through space, and every position along the drag creates a cross-section of the cube

payload.learning_objective: Understand that dragging a square perpendicular to its plane creates a cube with three dimensions. The aha moment: the cube is built by sweeping the square through space, and every position along the drag creates a cross-section of the cube.

mini_spec.title: Drag Square Handle to Extrude Cube
mini_spec.narrative_wrapper: A flat square sits on the screen with a handle or arrow pointing outward from its center. As you drag the handle away from the square, a 3D cube grows in real-time, stretching from the original square. Release to see the final cube with its dimensions labeled.
mini_spec.manipulated_variable: Handle or arrow extending perpendicular from square's center; controls extrusion depth/distance
mini_spec.primary_visual_feedback: Semi-transparent cube volume appears between original square and dragged position, with connecting edges drawing from original square's corners to new position; grows continuously during drag
mini_spec.causal_chain_exposed: Distance value updates numerically; intermediate rectangular faces forming the sides are visible with subtle shading; original square remains at starting position while copied square moves
mini_spec.trace_strategy: Ghost images of the square at previous positions fade gradually, showing the path of extrusion; original square remains solid to emphasize it as the base
mini_spec.aha_moment: The cube isn't just appearing — it's being built by sweeping the square through space, and every position along the drag creates a cross-section of the cube

payload.target_misconception: Students may struggle to visualize pulling a flat square into depth on a 2D screen
payload.section_context: Fourth section. Students have seen point → line → square. Now they see square → cube. This is the last familiar 3D shape before the leap to 4D tesseract.
payload.design_brief: This is a lesson about building a tesseract from the ground up. The shared domain model in src/data/model.ts contains functions for counting vertices/edges/faces/cells at each dimension and for 3D/4D projections. The tone is curious and exploratory, aimed at secondary school students. Each section reveals how dragging/moving a shape perpendicular creates the next dimension. Use the muted color palette (amber #F7B23B for this section). This section shows: square (2D) → cube (3D) through perpendicular extrusion. This is the last familiar dimension before 4D.
